title: The Mango

jokes.text: Scientists working on seedless watermelon while other scientists fight cancer and AIDS — 'No, I'm focusing on melon.'
jokes.setup: Jerry notes that scientists are devoting their lives to the seedless watermelon.
jokes.punchline: 'No, I'm focusing on melon. Oh, sure, thousands of people are dying needlessly...but this, that's gotta stop.'
jokes.explanation: A well-crafted observational bit. The juxtaposition of cancer/AIDS research against melon de-seeding is sharp and the imagined scientist's voice adds character. Solid stand-up writing with good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Seedless watermelon. Now that's a scientific achievement. We can put a man on the moon, but we can't figure out how to make a watermelon without seeds. And when they finally do it, what do they do? They act like they just cured cancer. 'Hey, look at us, we made a seedless watermelon!' Meanwhile, people are dying of AIDS.

jokes.text: You ever try and pick a wet one up off the floor? It's almost impossible.
jokes.setup: The watermelon seed discussion continues.
jokes.punchline: The absurdly specific and relatable physical challenge of a slippery watermelon slice.
jokes.explanation: A brief but functional observational aside. Ultra-economical but not a standout — it's a transitional joke that keeps the bit moving rather than landing a big laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And another thing about watermelon seeds - they're always wet. You're trying to spit them out, but your mouth is all wet, so the seed just kind of... slides around in there like you're playing some kind of disgusting game of pinball.

jokes.text: They're not gonna stop until they're making ready-to-eat fruit cups growing right out of the ground.
jokes.setup: If scientists can eliminate seeds and rind, what's left?
jokes.punchline: The logical extreme: pre-packaged fruit cups growing directly from the earth.
jokes.explanation: A satisfying escalation to close the watermelon bit. The image of a fruit cup growing from the ground is a good absurdist payoff for the preceding logical chain. Well-constructed closing image for the bit.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Scientists working on seeds, they're trying to eliminate the rind problem. You know what the logical progression is? Next thing you know, they're gonna be working on eliminating the rind on watermelons. Then they'll move on to oranges. Pretty soon, they'll have eliminated all the rinds. And then what? Then we'll have seedless, rindless fruit. We'll just be eating fruit pulp.

jokes.text: Jerry's theory: doing the tub (on your knees, Ajax, scrubbing) means you're in love.
jokes.setup: Jerry asks if George is cleaning up when Karen comes over — specifically if he does the tub.
jokes.punchline: 'Okay. I think you're in love.' The tub scrubbing on knees as the definitive love metric.
jokes.explanation: A genuinely original observational premise — using bathroom hygiene behavior as a love diagnostic — delivered with great specificity (on your knees, Ajax, hands, scrubbing). The buildup of details makes the declarative punchline 'Okay. I think you're in love' land perfectly. Highly quotable and feels true in a Seinfeld way.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So they're eliminating all the components of fruit. The skin, the pit, the seeds. I mean, what's left? Pre-packaged fruit cups from trees.

jokes.text: 'Tub is love.' / 'Tub is love.'
jokes.setup: Jerry just delivered the tub-equals-love theory.
jokes.punchline: The call-and-response confirmation reduces the entire romantic premise to two words: 'Tub is love.'
jokes.explanation: The distillation of the prior joke into three syllables is a perfect comedy move. 'Tub is love' echoed back and forth is maximally economical, instantly quotable, and has the rhythm of a mantra. It earns the follow-up laugh by reframing the entire preceding exchange as a philosophical statement.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're telling me you scrubbed the bathtub?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I scrubbed it!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You never scrub the bathtub.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I know!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You must really like this Karen.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I do. I really do.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A man scrubs the tub, that's love. That's the real thing right there.

jokes.text: 'Below the equator?' / 'Yeah.'
jokes.setup: George admits he's not confident in 'one particular aspect' of sex, dancing around the topic for 30 seconds.
jokes.punchline: Jerry cuts through the fumbling with the geographical euphemism 'Below the equator?' — George confirms.
jokes.explanation: The elegance of using a geographic euphemism for oral sex — 'below the equator' — is clever, economical, and classy. The extended awkward fumbling from George makes the precision of Jerry's cut-through all the funnier. A very Seinfeld-esque coinage.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, I think we've been overcomplicating this whole thing. At the end of the day, it all comes down to three words: clean the tub.

jokes.text: 'Nobody does. You know, nobody knows what to do. You just close your eyes, you hope for the best.'
jokes.setup: George confesses inadequacy in sexual performance 'below the equator.'
jokes.punchline: Jerry's reassurance is itself deflating: nobody knows, just close your eyes and hope.
jokes.explanation: A functionally funny deflation of George's anxiety. The 'nobody knows' reassurance being itself an admission of total ignorance is a nice irony. Good economy, but not quite a standout line.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's not you, it's me. I'm going through some things right now.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Like what?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I don't know... I just feel like I'm losing my edge.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Your edge? George, you never had an edge. You're like Pennsylvania.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What's wrong with Pennsylvania?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Nothing. It's exactly in the middle. Nobody gets excited about Pennsylvania.

jokes.text: 'Last time I got the tap.'
jokes.setup: Jerry says people are happy if you just make an effort.
jokes.punchline: George reveals he received 'the tap' — a physical signal to stop — presented with the gravity of a war wound.
jokes.explanation: An exceptional, original coinage. 'The tap' as a named phenomenon for being signaled to stop during oral sex is exactly the kind of specific, universal-yet-never-named concept that Seinfeld excels at. George's delivery of it as something catastrophic makes it perfect character comedy. Highly quotable, original, and deeply specific to George's personality.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what the difference is between men and women? Men don't know what they're doing and women know men don't know what they're doing.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So there's this gap of panic where men are just hoping nobody finds out. That's why men pray. 'Please God, don't let her notice I have no idea what I'm doing.'

jokes.text: The tap is like 'the manager coming out and asking you for the ball.'
jokes.setup: The tap as a dismissal signal is established.
jokes.punchline: The baseball pitcher analogy — manager pulling you from the game — for being signaled to stop during sex.
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines in the episode. The baseball pitcher analogy is brilliantly constructed: the manager walking out, asking for the ball — the humiliation and finality of it perfectly mirrors the tap. It's an earned comparison that illuminates the original concept from a new angle. Highly quotable, original, and the sports metaphor makes it universally accessible to the male audience.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's not a lie if you believe it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, it's all about confidence. You just gotta sell it.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: The tap!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What tap?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You gotta use the tap. Light little tap on the back of the head.

jokes.text: 'I got the hook.'
jokes.setup: Jerry suggests she might have wanted to 'move on to other business.'
jokes.punchline: George's correction: not moving on — 'I got the hook' — invoking the vaudeville cane pulling bad acts offstage.
jokes.explanation: George escalating from 'the tap' to 'the hook' (the vaudeville hook for bad performers) is a superb escalation. It deepens his humiliation while maintaining the extended performance metaphor. George's matter-of-fact delivery of his own degradation is pure character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: The tap! The tap!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What tap?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: The tap on the table. It's a tap!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A tap? What are you talking about?

jokes.text: 'It's a hazy mystery.'
jokes.setup: Jerry says it's a very complicated area, you could go crazy trying to figure it out.
jokes.punchline: 'It's a hazy mystery' — the ultimate non-explanation that's also completely accurate.
jokes.explanation: A pithy, economical line that captures male bewilderment in three words. Not a major laugh but it's the kind of perfectly worded aside that elevates Seinfeld dialogue.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's like a manager taking a player out of the game. He's gotta do it, but nobody likes it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what are you, the manager?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm the player! She's taking me out of the game!

jokes.text: George casually drops 'unless, of course, she's faking' — which pivots the entire conversation and explodes in his face when Elaine reveals she faked with Jerry.
jokes.setup: The conversation has been about George's inadequacy and 'the tap.'
jokes.punchline: George's throwaway 'unless she's faking' triggers Elaine's devastating admission about Jerry.
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in structural comedy. George's casual mention of faking is intended to deflect from his own problems but accidentally opens a Pandora's box. The way the topic ricochets from George's concerns to Elaine's confession about Jerry is the kind of precise plotting that makes Seinfeld great. The structural pivot is the joke as much as any single line.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I can tell. I can always tell.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh, come on.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, I'm telling you, I know. It's like a sixth sense. I can feel it.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You know, it's not that hard to fake.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Not for you to fake, no. But I can tell.

jokes.text: 'It's one of my powers.' (Jerry claims he can always tell when a woman fakes.)
jokes.setup: Jerry says he knows Elaine isn't faking her orgasms, and George asks how he knows.
jokes.punchline: 'It's one of my powers.' — Delivered with complete sincerity, setting up the imminent humiliation.
jokes.explanation: The line lands on two levels: immediately funny as a confident absurd claim, and then retrospectively hilarious once Elaine confirms she faked with Jerry. 'One of my powers' is exactly the kind of unearned self-confidence that makes Jerry's character tick. It becomes a devastating setup for the punchline that follows moments later.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: It's not that big a deal.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Not that big a deal? Do you know how much pressure that is on us? That's all we care about!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, it's easy. I just fake it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You fake it?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Fake it? You don't fake it?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh, all the time.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Sure. It's not a big thing.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Not a big thing? How do you fake that?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh, it's easy. The breathing gets heavier, you moan a little bit, and then you say dirty things.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Dirty things?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh, you know. 'Oh, that's so good, oh that's so good.'

jokes.text: Elaine admits she has faked orgasms — and reveals she faked with Jerry specifically.
jokes.setup: Jerry has just declared 'it's one of my powers' to detect faking.
jokes.punchline: Elaine casually confirms she has faked, then the room-clearing implication that she faked with Jerry.
jokes.explanation: The episode's central comedic bomb. The slow-burn from 'Why? Did you ever fake?' to Elaine's casual 'Of course' to the dawning horror of 'with me?' is exquisitely structured. It destroys Jerry's earlier claim of superpower detection in real time. A 9+ moment by craft — the structural payoff is exceptional and the implications cascade throughout the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait, you're telling me you've never seen Breakfast at Tiffany's?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No, I haven't.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You've never seen Breakfast at Tiffany's?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Elaine, it's a classic! Audrey Hepburn!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I know who's in it.

jokes.text: 'I guess after that many beers, he's probably a little groggy anyway.' (George's aside about the guy Elaine faked with.)
jokes.setup: Elaine says the guy never knew she was faking because she was good.
jokes.punchline: George's deflating addition: the guy was probably too drunk to notice.
jokes.explanation: A functional side joke — George's cynical gloss on male obliviousness — but it's a fairly predictable addition. The beer/grogginess logic is a bit lazy compared to the sharper material around it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I can't believe you fooled me! I trusted you!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What can I say? I'm a natural.

jokes.text: Jerry processes that he didn't know Elaine was faking — the camera/audience reaction beat as the implication sinks in.
jokes.setup: Elaine has admitted to faking. George points out 'You didn't know.'
jokes.punchline: Jerry's stunned silent reaction — then George's cutting 'You didn't know' lands like a verdict.
jokes.explanation: The comedic pause followed by George's devastating 'You didn't know' is a perfectly executed reaction beat. Jerry's silence does the heavy lifting — the 'powers' claim now hangs in the air demolished. The economy of George's three words as the punchline is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Look, I have all the evidence right here. The whole thing makes perfect sense.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Fake.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What do you mean fake? I have documentation!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Fake.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: But the witnesses—

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Fake.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: The photographs are real!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Fake.

jokes.text: 'I think I'll have a piece of cake.' (Elaine changing subject mid-confrontation.)
jokes.setup: George's 'You didn't know' has just landed. Everyone is stunned. Elaine is the one on the hot seat.
jokes.punchline: Elaine's non-sequitur pivot to dessert to escape the moment.
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly economical escape move. Elaine's casual pivot to cake in the middle of a sexually awkward confrontation is a perfect character beat — she's guilty but unruffled. The word 'cake' as the punchline to a charged silence is maximum economy comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm out there, Jerry!

jokes.text: 'That whole thing, the whole production, it was all an act?'
jokes.setup: Jerry is confronting Elaine about faking with him.
jokes.punchline: 'That whole thing, the whole production' — Jerry's incredulous framing of her performance as theatrical production.
jokes.explanation: Jerry calling it 'a whole production' is funny for its theatrical inflation, and it sets up the next line well. Character-specific outrage from a man whose ego is fundamentally invested in this.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what I hate? When someone lies to you about their sexual experience. It's like false advertising. You think you're getting one thing, you're getting something completely different. It should be illegal!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm serious! There should be a law. 'Misrepresentation of Sexual Capability' — it's fraud!

jokes.text: 'Not bad, huh?' (Elaine's proud response to Jerry's 'It was all an act?')
jokes.setup: Jerry is devastated that Elaine faked 'the whole production.'
jokes.punchline: Elaine's smug, self-congratulatory 'Not bad, huh?' — taking credit for the deception as a performance achievement.
jokes.explanation: Three words that are as perfectly Elaine as anything she says in the whole series. She doesn't apologize — she's proud of the performance. The pride she takes in deceiving her friend/ex-lover is darkly funny, sharply in-character, and deeply subversive. Maximum economy, maximum character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, she's very good. She had me completely fooled.

jokes.text: 'What about the breathing, the panting, the moaning, the screaming?' / 'Fake, fake, fake, fake.'
jokes.setup: Jerry is cataloguing the components of Elaine's faked response.
jokes.punchline: Elaine's deadpan four-word demolition: 'Fake, fake, fake, fake.' — one for each item Jerry listed.
jokes.explanation: The quadruple callback rhythm 'Fake, fake, fake, fake' is a comedy writing clinic. The structure is perfect: Jerry's list of four items met with exactly four 'fakes.' The rhythm, the finality, the casual delivery — this is among the most quotable lines of the episode. A landmark moment.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, you're a pretty good actress.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I mean, you really had me convinced. That's the kind of performance I'd like to see in the bedroom.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm saying you should bring that same level of commitment and intensity to your intimate moments that you bring to your acting.

jokes.text: 'All the time.' / 'All the time?'
jokes.setup: Jerry asks how many times Elaine faked with him.
jokes.punchline: 'All the time.' — the absolute worst possible answer, delivered without hesitation.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'a few times' to 'all the time' is the comedic gut punch. Elaine's zero-hesitation delivery makes it worse. Jerry's echo ('All the time?') with presumably rising horror is the reaction beat. Three syllables that destroy Jerry's self-image completely.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You gotta treat it like a button. You just gotta find the right button.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's not a button, Kramer.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Well, what is it?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't know, it's not a button!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Well, you're pressing it like it's a button.

jokes.text: 'I think I'll have a chocolate malted here.' (George changing subject again while Jerry melts down.)
jokes.setup: Jerry is spiraling in existential horror at 'All the time.'
jokes.punchline: George's studied indifference — pivoting to a dessert order in the middle of Jerry's devastation.
jokes.explanation: A callback to Elaine's 'I think I'll have a piece of cake' escape move — now George does the same thing. Works well as a running gag but is slightly less surprising the second time. Still earns the laugh by reinforcing the absurdity of their social dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Do you ever fake it?

dialogue.speaker: Renee
dialogue.line: Fake it? I don't know what you mean.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, fake it.

dialogue.speaker: Renee
dialogue.line: Oh! You mean like when I go to a Broadway show and pretend to enjoy it?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, no, no...

jokes.text: 'But I'm so good.' / 'I'm sure you are.'
jokes.setup: Jerry is reeling from 'All the time' and grasping for self-esteem.
jokes.punchline: Elaine's perfectly modulated 'I'm sure you are' — a condescending consolation prize that confirms nothing.
jokes.explanation: Elaine's placating non-answer is exactly right. 'I'm sure you are' is the verbal pat on the head — it neither confirms nor denies, which is somehow worse than a flat denial. Her tone is perfectly calibrated: kind but devastating.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Women fake it all the time!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Oh, men fake it too.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Men fake what?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I don't know... interest. Happiness. Life satisfaction.

jokes.text: Jerry silently processing — 'She faked.' (Talking-head or private reaction beat, delivered to camera/George off-screen.)
jokes.setup: Elaine has departed. Jerry is left with the wreckage of his self-image.
jokes.punchline: The flat, quiet 'She faked.' — summarizing an entire emotional collapse in two words.
jokes.explanation: Jerry's two-word post-mortem captures everything. The flatness is the joke — the epic scale of his devastation reduced to the simplest possible statement. Great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: These are the worst bananas I've ever seen! Look at them! They're all brown and spotty!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Kramer, they're from the fruit bowl. What do you expect?

jokes.text: 'Maybe they've all been faking.'
jokes.setup: Jerry is unraveling, and Elaine tries to reassure him.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's paranoia spiral — if Elaine faked, maybe ALL women have faked with him.
jokes.explanation: The classic neurotic escalation — from a single data point to total catastrophizing. Very Jerry, very Seinfeld. Sets up the later phone-call payoff perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, this peach is subpar. I demand restitution.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Restitution? It's a peach, Kramer.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I don't care. This is unacceptable fruit.

jokes.text: 'The woman had an orgasm under false pretenses. That's sexual perjury.'
jokes.setup: George challenges why faking orgasms is a big deal.
jokes.punchline: 'Sexual perjury' — a legal coinage for false orgasms.
jokes.explanation: 'Sexual perjury' is a genuine neologism — the legal framework applied to fake orgasms is original, perfectly economical, and deeply quotable. This is the kind of word-coinage Seinfeld is famous for. Strong candidate for the episode's most quotable line.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: This peach is mealy! I want a refund!

dialogue.speaker: Joe
dialogue.line: A mealy peach is still a peach. God doesn't make mistakes with fruit.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Oh, so God is responsible for this?

dialogue.speaker: Joe
dialogue.line: Everything that comes from the earth is a gift from above. You don't question the gift.

jokes.text: 'You know, I heard her screaming from my apartment. She woke me up a few times.'
jokes.setup: Jerry is upset about being deceived. George is offering context.
jokes.punchline: George casually revealing he could hear Jerry and Elaine having (fake) sex from his apartment — and that it disturbed his sleep.
jokes.explanation: A devastating detail: George complaining about being woken up by Jerry and Elaine's sex (which was faked) is deeply funny on multiple levels. George's framing — as a neighbor grievance rather than anything else — is perfect character comedy. The mundane complaint about sleep disruption is a brilliant angle.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: They banned me from the fruit store. Me! Can you believe that?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What did you do?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I don't know! I went in, I picked up a mango, I squeezed it a little bit...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You squeezed it?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, to see if it was ripe! That's what you do with mangoes!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Now I can't go back there. Where am I supposed to get my fruit, Jerry? Where?

jokes.text: 'How did she do it? She's like Meryl Streep, this woman.'
jokes.setup: George mentions being woken by Elaine's convincing performance.
jokes.punchline: Jerry comparing Elaine's fake orgasm to Meryl Streep's acting — simultaneously complimenting and condemning her.
jokes.explanation: The Meryl Streep comparison plants a seed for a callback later in the episode (Jerry's discomfort when Karen praises Meryl Streep as 'authentic'). A good joke on its own that becomes structurally important.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So was it good for you?

dialogue.speaker: Karen
dialogue.line: You're an enigma, Jerry.

jokes.text: 'And I know how to work the equipment. I'm not unskilled, I'm in the union.'
jokes.setup: Jerry is defending his sexual competence after being told his partner faked.
jokes.punchline: 'I'm in the union' — the labor metaphor for sexual skill.
jokes.explanation: The labor/union metaphor for sexual competence is an original and funny construction. 'I'm in the union' as a credential for bedroom skills is an absurdist but earned extension of the 'equipment/skilled worker' framing.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's like when you go to a restaurant and you order something, and it comes out and it's not what you expected. You're disappointed.

dialogue.speaker: Karen
dialogue.line: So you send it back.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, you can't send back sex.

dialogue.speaker: Karen
dialogue.line: Why not? If it's not what you ordered, send it back.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, I could've helped you out.' / 'What could you have done?' / 'I could've given you some pointers. I know how to press those buttons, buddy.'
jokes.setup: Jerry says if Elaine had told him she wasn't satisfied, he could have done something.
jokes.punchline: George confidently offering to have given Jerry 'pointers' — implying his own superior knowledge of female pleasure.
jokes.explanation: George's confident claim to sexual expertise is funny given his established inadequacy (he just got 'the hook'). The irony of the worst possible advisor offering tips is solid character comedy, even if the joke doesn't fully land.

dialogue.speaker: Karen
dialogue.line: Meryl Streep is so authentic, so real in her performances.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, well she's also a very good faker.

jokes.text: Jerry says he doesn't want to see Elaine. She calls. He claims he's upset about 'the grilled cheese — they always burn the toast.'
jokes.setup: Jerry is upset about being told he was faked with. Elaine calls to check on him.
jokes.punchline: Jerry deflects with 'What, the grilled cheese? No, they always burn the toast.' — a transparently fake cover story.
jokes.explanation: Jerry's bad cover story is a light, functional joke. The specificity of the grilled cheese complaint makes it funnier than a generic deflection, but it's transitional comedy rather than a standout moment.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I need another chance. I'm telling you, I can do better. Just give me one more shot.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Jerry, it's not about getting another chance. It's about what happened.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I know what happened! And I'm saying it was a fluke. A complete anomaly. I'm usually great at this.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You're not getting it. You can't just redo it like it's a scene in a play.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why not? Athletes get replays. Why can't I get a replay?

jokes.text: George asks his girlfriend Renee if she's ever faked. She says yes — 'If we went to a Broadway show...if we had really good seats.'
jokes.setup: George, infected by the faking paranoia, asks Renee if she's ever faked.
jokes.punchline: Renee's completely unexpected condition: she faked only when they had really good Broadway seats. The non-sequitur logic is baffling and funny.
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly weird non-sequitur: the connection between good Broadway seats and faking orgasms is absurdist and inexplicable in the best possible way. The audience is left trying to construct the logic (was she too tired? too happy? did the cultural high transfer?), and the confusion is the joke. Highly original.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What, a relationship would ruin our friendship? I think it would enhance our friendship.

jokes.text: 'You know, if it's enough already, and I just wanna get some sleep.'
jokes.setup: Renee is explaining the other conditions under which she fakes.
jokes.punchline: The brutally casual 'if it's enough already, and I just wanna get some sleep' — orgasm-faking as a convenience tool.
jokes.explanation: The pragmatic framing of faking as sleep management is a funny angle. Works best in contrast with the Broadway seats line — this is the mundane version of the same confession. Sets up the callback when George repeats these exact words.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Come on, just give me another chance. I can change.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: How much time do you need?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't know, a week?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: A week? That's too long. I can do three days.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Three days? Come on, split the difference — five days.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Four days. Final offer.

jokes.text: Kramer comes in eating a peach, dramatically spitting it out: 'Bad peach? It's terrible!'
jokes.setup: The scene shifts to Kramer, who is eating fruit.
jokes.punchline: The extreme disgust reaction to a bad peach — Kramer's signature dramatic overreaction.
jokes.explanation: A functional scene-opener. Kramer's physical reactions to the bad peach are good Kramer comedy but not exceptional. The real comedy in this subplot emerges from Joe the fruit store owner.

jokes.text: Kramer demands restitution for the bad peach. Joe: 'Once I put it out, it ends for me.' Kramer: 'You gotta stand behind your fruit.'
jokes.setup: Kramer tries to return a bad peach to Joe's fruit stand.
jokes.punchline: The formal legal/business language ('restitution,' 'stand behind your fruit') applied to a 59-cent piece of fruit.
jokes.explanation: The mock-legalistic argument over a piece of fruit is funny because of the disproportionate stakes. 'You gotta stand behind your fruit' as a moral principle is a good Kramer line. Joe's indifference is the perfect foil.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, well Meryl Streep can go take a nap!

jokes.text: Joe: 'You got a bad peach, that's an act of God. He makes the peaches. I sell the peaches. You got a problem, you talk to him.'
jokes.setup: Kramer demands restitution for the bad peach.
jokes.punchline: Joe deflects responsibility to God — the theological defense against produce quality control.
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines in the episode. Joe's theological liability shield — God made the peach, Joe just sells it, take your complaint upstairs — is a brilliantly original piece of absurdist logic delivered with complete conviction. The three-part structure (God makes, I sell, talk to him) is perfectly economical.

dialogue.speaker: Karen
dialogue.line: I'm upset.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, even your emotions are fake.

jokes.text: 'You know, this whole place is going downhill. I could've come in here last week with a bad plum, but I let it go.'
jokes.setup: Joe refuses to give restitution.
jokes.punchline: Kramer escalating the grievance by revealing a previous bad plum he 'let go' — framing it as generosity that is now exhausted.
jokes.explanation: Kramer invoking the bad plum he magnanimously didn't complain about is a funny escalation. 'But I let it go' suggests Kramer has been keeping a grievance ledger on this fruit stand. Good character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Come on, work with me here. Just this once. I'm begging you.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, it's like he's not listening to you.

jokes.text: 'Take your business elsewhere. I don't want your business.' / 'Now you don't want my business?' / 'I don't. And at this moment, you're banned from this store. You're banned!'
jokes.setup: The peach argument escalates beyond all proportion.
jokes.punchline: Joe banning Kramer from the store over a bad peach — nuclear response to a petty dispute.
jokes.explanation: The banning is a funny escalation and sets up the running gag of people being banned from Joe's store. Joe's righteous fury over produce quality control works well.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, it's like being a magician. Sometimes the trick works, sometimes it doesn't. But the key is, you gotta believe in yourself.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I don't want to be a magician, Jerry. I want to be a man!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You are a man. You're just a man who occasionally needs a little more time to escape from the locked box.

jokes.text: 'But what am I gonna do for fruit?'
jokes.setup: Kramer has just been banned from the only good fruit store.
jokes.punchline: Kramer's devastated, plaintive 'But what am I gonna do for fruit?' — reacting to the ban as an existential crisis.
jokes.explanation: Kramer's devastation is perfectly in character. The existential weight he places on fruit access is funny, and the line's simplicity gives it punch. Also establishes the stakes that drive the subplot forward.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, it's funny. We spend all this time worried about psychic phenomena, ESP, telekinesis... But you know what's harder to achieve? An erection. That's right. I'd rather move a table with my mind than get it up on a Tuesday afternoon.

jokes.text: Jerry reading sexual satisfaction into Karen's enjoyment of risotto: 'You have a very contented air over there. You look very contented, very satisfied. Are you satisfied?'
jokes.setup: Jerry is at dinner with Karen and can't stop wondering if she's faking
jokes.punchline: His displacement of sexual anxiety onto the risotto — interrogating her food satisfaction as a proxy for sexual satisfaction
jokes.explanation: Jerry using food-satisfaction language as displacement for sexual anxiety is both funny and character-revealing. The escalation of 'contented' appearing twice before 'satisfied' shows how hard he's working not to ask the real question. The risotto becomes a running metaphor for the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I can't shop at the supermarket anymore. Last week I bought some apples, they were mealy. The week before that, oranges, they were dry. And then I got some papayas - I don't even know what a papaya is supposed to taste like!

jokes.text: Jerry asks Karen 'Are you satisfied?' after dinner. 'I'm very satisfied.' 'I'm sure if you weren't satisfied, you would say something, wouldn't you?' 'I probably would.' 'But then again, I'm an enigma.'
jokes.setup: Jerry is on a date with Karen and fishing for validation after Elaine's faking confession.
jokes.punchline: Karen's 'But then again, I'm an enigma' — she names her own inscrutability as a trait, making Jerry's paranoia worse.
jokes.explanation: A superb cringe comedy moment. Karen's 'I'm an enigma' turns every reassurance she just gave Jerry into a question mark. It's both funny and deeply unsettling for his character, and it shows self-awareness that makes it funnier than if she'd been oblivious. Perfectly timed.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm calling all my ex-girlfriends to ask them one simple question.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Did you ever fake it with me?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I need to know the truth. I'm conducting a comprehensive survey.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Hello? Yeah, it's Jerry. Listen, I have a very important question for you...

jokes.text: Jerry asks Karen if she feels 'the way you feel after the risotto.' 'Well, no. I feel full after the risotto.'
jokes.setup: Jerry is trying to ask Karen about sexual satisfaction using the risotto as a metaphor.
jokes.punchline: Karen takes the comparison literally — she feels FULL after risotto, not satisfied. The metaphor collapses completely.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed cringe moment. Jerry's fumbling attempt to probe Karen's sexual satisfaction using food as a euphemism is already uncomfortable, then Karen's literal interpretation ('I feel full after the risotto') deflates the whole endeavor. The deflation is the exact right beat.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about getting Kramer some fruit.

dialogue.speaker: Joe
dialogue.line: Fruit? What kind of fruit?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't know, whatever he likes.

dialogue.speaker: Joe
dialogue.line: Well, you're going to have to do some detective work. Find out what his preferences are.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Detective work? It's fruit, not a murder investigation.

dialogue.speaker: Joe
dialogue.line: Everything requires detective work. You think people just randomly like things? No. There's a method. There's a system.

jokes.text: Jerry's 'Oh, God.' reaction beat after Karen's risotto response.
jokes.setup: Karen's risotto answer has confirmed that Jerry's clumsy questioning has gotten nowhere.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's barely-suppressed existential collapse: 'Oh, God.'
jokes.explanation: The two-word defeat is functionally funny. Jerry's compression of his entire romantic paranoia into 'Oh, God.' is good but fairly standard reaction beat territory.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, this mango is unbelievable. I mean, this is the best mango I've ever had in my life.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't know what it is about this mango, but I feel... I feel like I could do anything right now.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I've got energy! I've got focus! I feel like I could run through a wall!

jokes.text: Jerry asks 'Satisfied?' post-sex. Karen immediately suggests seeing the new Meryl Streep movie. Jerry recoils.
jokes.setup: Post-sex, Jerry fishes for sexual satisfaction feedback.
jokes.punchline: Karen pivots immediately to Meryl Streep — a callback to the 'she's like Meryl Streep' fake orgasm comparison, now landing with full cringe impact.
jokes.explanation: A brilliant structural callback. Karen's innocent Meryl Streep mention immediately after sex — when Jerry has already coded Meryl Streep as 'world-class faker' — is the kind of layered joke Seinfeld excels at. The audience gets the joke; Karen is oblivious. Perfect cringe architecture.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's back. It's back!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What's back?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's back. My confidence. My mojo. It's all back!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yes! I'm back, baby!

jokes.text: 'God, I love her, Jerry. She's so authentic. You believe everything is actually happening to her. No acting there.'
jokes.setup: Jerry visibly tenses at the Meryl Streep mention.
jokes.punchline: Karen praising Meryl Streep's authenticity — 'no acting there' — to a man who just coined the term 'Meryl Streep' for fake orgasms. The dramatic irony is perfect.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional structural irony. Karen unknowingly twisting the knife by celebrating Meryl Streep's authenticity — the very quality Jerry has associated with his own sexual inadequacy — is a textbook example of Seinfeld's layered comedy. 'No acting there' is devastating in context. The audience laughs, Jerry squirms.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No, no, no. Sex would ruin the friendship.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, what if we had sex to save the friendship?

jokes.text: Jerry asks Elaine for 'another shot' — he wants to redo their sex life to prove himself.
jokes.setup: Jerry is consumed by the need to prove he can make Elaine have a real orgasm.
jokes.punchline: Jerry asking for 'another shot' as if he's asking for another at-bat — the male ego reduction to a sports/competition framework.
jokes.explanation: The 'another shot' framing is spot-on Jerry — the ego reduction of intimacy to a performance metric. The earnestness of his 'One shot. I could do it. I know I could do it' makes it funnier and more pathetic.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You were faking it.

dialogue.speaker: Karen
dialogue.line: I was not faking it.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You were faking it. I know faking when I see it.

dialogue.speaker: Karen
dialogue.line: George, I wasn't faking.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Oh, come on. You were faking the whole thing.

jokes.text: 'Oh, friendship. Friendship, schmiendship.'
jokes.setup: Elaine says they can't have sex because it would ruin their friendship.
jokes.punchline: Jerry dismisses 'friendship' with the Yiddish schm- prefix dismissal: 'schmiendship.'
jokes.explanation: The Yiddish dismissal prefix is a classic Seinfeld comedy device (schm-reduplication). 'Schmiendship' is a fun coinage but leans on a familiar bit of New York comedy vocabulary rather than something entirely original.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You were faking it! I know you were faking it!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Oh! Oh! Oh! Yes! Yes! Yes! That's it! That's it!

jokes.text: Jerry's rapid negotiation: 'Half-hour. Give me a half-hour.' / 'No.' / 'Okay, 15 minutes. I guarantee you, 15 minutes, I can make it happen.'
jokes.setup: Elaine refuses to have sex with Jerry.
jokes.punchline: Jerry immediately halving his time commitment — negotiating his sexual window like a used car deal, down to 15 minutes with a guarantee.
jokes.explanation: The negotiation is both charming and pathetic in exactly the right proportions. Jerry's willingness to bid himself down to 15 minutes and offer a guarantee shows his ego is simultaneously enormous (he's sure he can do it) and wounded (15 minutes is not a generous offer). Excellent character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Women, they're like the Batcave. You know where the entrance is, but once you're inside, you have no idea where you're going. You're just stumbling around in the dark, hoping you don't accidentally activate some elaborate security system.

jokes.text: 'No, that's it. That's it. You like having this over me. You don't want me to do it.'
jokes.setup: Elaine says she doesn't care whether Jerry can make it happen.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's paranoid pivot: Elaine doesn't want him to succeed because she LIKES having the faking over him as leverage.
jokes.explanation: Jerry's conspiracy theory about Elaine's motives is funny because it's so specifically neurotic and so plausibly wrong — and yet the show allows for the possibility he might be right. Good character beat but not a standout line.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, sex is like a car accident. It happens so fast, you don't even see it coming. One minute you're driving along, minding your own business, the next thing you know — CRASH! It's over.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And just like a car accident, there's a lot of paperwork involved. You gotta fill out a report. 'Where were you going? What was your speed? Did you see the other party approaching? Were there any witnesses?'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And the worst part — just like a car accident — your insurance rates go up!

jokes.text: George enters Karen's apartment having clearly performed poorly. 'It's Jerry's fault.' / Karen: 'Jerry?' George: 'Jerry and Elaine. They made me nuts.'
jokes.setup: The episode has set up George's anxiety about performance due to Elaine's faking revelation.
jokes.punchline: George blaming his sexual failure on Jerry and Elaine's orgasm conversation — externalizing responsibility in classic George fashion.
jokes.explanation: Quintessential George — deflecting blame for personal failure onto external parties. His impotence is Jerry and Elaine's fault because of the orgasm conversation. The complete refusal of self-accountability is pitch-perfect George character comedy.

jokes.text: Karen says she's okay, 'Well, it's not like after the risotto.'
jokes.setup: George is asking Karen if she's okay after his failed performance.
jokes.punchline: Karen echoes the risotto metaphor from Jerry's dinner scene — 'not like after the risotto' — bringing the food-as-satisfaction motif back with new meaning.
jokes.explanation: A superb structural callback. The risotto metaphor migrates from Jerry's insecure dinner conversation to Karen's post-George assessment — confirming George also failed to satisfy. The shared metaphor across the episode's two storylines is elegant comedy writing.

jokes.text: 'Oh, what, you're upset? Yes, I'm upset. Can't you tell?' / 'No, I can't. Maybe you're faking.'
jokes.setup: Jerry and Elaine leave the movie (Meryl Streep) early and she's upset.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's accusation that Elaine might be faking her upset emotions — extending the faking theme to non-sexual feelings.
jokes.explanation: Extending the faking motif to emotional expression is a clever callback. Jerry turning Elaine's sexual deception into a general principle ('maybe you're faking' all feelings) is both funny and a good character beat — the paranoia has spread. Highly quotable and earns big laughs.

jokes.text: George complains that Meryl Streep is 'such a phony baloney' — an echo of the faking/authentic debate.
jokes.setup: George and Karen walk out of the Meryl Streep movie.
jokes.punchline: George calling Meryl Streep a 'phony baloney' — ironic given the episode's obsession with authentic vs. fake performance.
jokes.explanation: George calling Meryl Streep a 'phony baloney' after the episode's extended use of her as a metaphor for convincing fake performance is a good ironic callback. His reversal from Karen's praise of Meryl as 'authentic' is funny and thematically coherent.

jokes.text: George's stream of consciousness about impotence: 'I was talking to him. "Please wake up. Do something."'
jokes.setup: George is explaining his performance failure to Jerry — how the orgasm talk made him panic.
jokes.punchline: George literally talking to his own penis during the failed attempt: 'Please wake up. Do something.'
jokes.explanation: George's internal dialogue with his own anatomy is peak George. The pleading urgency of 'Please wake up. Do something.' is both hilarious and deeply pathetic — a man in full crisis talking to himself below the waist. Exceptional character integration.

jokes.text: 'They're mysterious little fellows, aren't they?' / 'I hate him.'
jokes.setup: Jerry is trying to empathize with George's impotence.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's detached, almost naturalistic 'mysterious little fellows' for male genitalia, followed by George's 'I hate him' — George blaming his penis as a separate entity.
jokes.explanation: A brilliant two-part exchange. Jerry's 'mysterious little fellows' — addressing the penis as a wildlife species — is original and funny. George's 'I hate him' completes it perfectly by confirming that George has indeed personified his own anatomy as a separate adversary to be hated. The sequence is quotable and deeply in-character.

jokes.text: 'You know, it happens to everybody. Happened to Houdini. And he could get out of a trunk with his hands in chains. But he had a problem with that.'
jokes.setup: Jerry is trying to comfort George about impotence.
jokes.punchline: Houdini — the greatest escape artist in history, could break free from any physical restraint — supposedly had impotence issues. The contrast is absurdist and perfectly calibrated.
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly original comparison. The juxtaposition of Houdini's supernatural physical abilities with impotence is unexpected, specific, and earns big laughs through pure contrast. 'He could get out of a trunk with his hands in chains. But he had a problem with that.' is impeccably constructed.

jokes.text: 'And he could get out of a trunk with his hands in chains. But he had a problem with that.'
jokes.setup: Houdini cited as a famous ED sufferer
jokes.punchline: The contrast between Houdini's miraculous escapes and his inability to perform sexually — 'but he had a problem with that' — is beautifully specific
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's peak jokes. The contrast between Houdini's supernatural escapes and his alleged ED is a structural gem — the juxtaposition of 'get out of a trunk with hands in chains' with 'but he had a problem with that' is perfectly constructed. The phrase 'but he had a problem with that' does all the work without naming the thing. Brilliant economy.

jokes.text: 'The miracle is that it ever happens.' / 'It's like a magic trick.' / 'Sometimes I think it would be easier to bend a spoon mentally than to make that transformation.'
jokes.setup: The Houdini comparison leads to a broader meditation on male sexual response.
jokes.punchline: Jerry: it would be easier to do telekinesis (bend a spoon mentally) than to achieve an erection on demand.
jokes.explanation: The Uri Geller 'bend a spoon mentally' comparison is a fun extension of the magic/miracle metaphor. Good but slightly less punchy than the Houdini line that preceded it.

jokes.text: Kramer asks Jerry to buy fruit for him because he's been banned. 'What am I gonna do for fruit?' Kramer can't go back.
jokes.setup: Kramer is banned from Joe's fruit stand.
jokes.punchline: The absurd situation where Kramer needs a fruit proxy — too proud to go to a supermarket, too banned to go to Joe's.
jokes.explanation: Functional callback to the store-ban subplot. Sets up Jerry's fruit-procurement mission but isn't a strong standalone joke.

jokes.text: Jerry creates a fruit list for Kramer: five mangoes, avocado, plums red on the inside, plantains. George protests each item.
jokes.setup: Kramer needs Jerry to buy fruit on his behalf. Jerry produces an elaborate list.
jokes.punchline: George's hapless objections to each item culminating in 'What's a plantain?' and Kramer's 'It's in the banana family. It's a delicacy.' 'You're not getting any plantains.'
jokes.explanation: The fruit list scene is a good escalating absurdist comedy bit. Kramer's insane specificity ('plums with red on the inside'), George's incompetence, and the final 'You're not getting any plantains' all work well. More enjoyable as a scene than any single line is quotable.

jokes.text: George at the fruit store nervously reciting 'Just getting some fruit for myself. Gotta have fruit in the house. I like it as a snack. Wholesome...natural, chock-full of vitamins.'
jokes.setup: George is undercover buying fruit for Kramer while pretending it's for himself.
jokes.punchline: George's over-elaborate unprompted alibi — nobody asked, but he volunteers that fruit is wholesome, natural, vitamin-rich.
jokes.explanation: Peak guilty-George behavior — the over-justification that reveals exactly what he's trying to hide. Nobody asked why he wants fruit, which makes his soliloquy about health benefits completely self-incriminating. Great character comedy.

jokes.text: Joe catches George: 'This fruit isn't for you. Mangoes, plantains, plums with the red on the inside? That's Kramer.'
jokes.setup: George is trying to buy fruit for Kramer while pretending it's his own order.
jokes.punchline: Joe has memorized Kramer's exact fruit preferences well enough to identify a proxy buyer — 'That's Kramer' — based on the shopping list.
jokes.explanation: A superb payoff to the fruit subplot. Joe's ability to identify Kramer from a shopping list is simultaneously absurd (who has that knowledge?) and completely plausible in context. 'That's Kramer' as the punchline — a name as the punchline — is structurally perfect. Great comic escalation.

jokes.text: 'Are you banning me from the store?' / 'That's exactly what I'm saying.' / 'I'm banned?' / 'You're banned.'
jokes.setup: Joe has caught George buying for Kramer.
jokes.punchline: George becomes the second person banned from Joe's store in the same episode — the running gag of bannings escalates.
jokes.explanation: The double-banning is a satisfying running gag escalation. George being banned for merely buying fruit on behalf of a banned man is an absurd extension of the premise. The echo of the 'You're banned' / 'I'm banned?' rhythm from the Kramer scene works well.

jokes.text: George tastes the mango: 'This mango is delicious.' Then immediately: 'That reminds me. I'm not getting you guys any more fruit.'
jokes.setup: George has successfully delivered the fruit and is admiring it.
jokes.punchline: The mango's deliciousness reminds George of his ordeal — the absurd non-sequitur logic of 'That reminds me: no more fruit.'
jokes.explanation: The non-sequitur connection — the delicious mango reminds him of the humiliation — is funny in its twisted logic. Good character moment, nice delivery.

jokes.text: 'Gee, sorry, I don't have any cash. I only got hundreds.'
jokes.setup: George demands $28.60 reimbursement for the fruit.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's passive-aggressive non-payment: 'I only got hundreds' — technically solvent but practically useless.
jokes.explanation: A functional joke about Jerry's passive cheapness. 'I only got hundreds' as a dodge is a well-known comedian's trick and not especially original here, but it's delivered well.

jokes.text: Kramer tries the mango: 'I feel like I got a B12 shot.' / 'This is like a taste explosion.' / 'I think it moved.'
jokes.setup: Kramer takes a bite of mango.
jokes.punchline: Kramer's cascade of increasingly hyperbolic reactions culminating in 'I think it moved' — implying the mango has cured his impotence.
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly structured escalation. The cascade from B12 shot to taste explosion to 'I think it moved' — the last item being Kramer's implicit impotence cure — is perfectly constructed. The third item's implication is funnier than if it were stated outright. Excellent Kramer moment.

jokes.text: George eats the mango, runs out immediately: 'I'm back, baby! I'm back!'
jokes.setup: George was suffering from impotence. He eats the mango.
jokes.punchline: 'I'm back, baby! I'm back!' — George's sexual resurrection, attributed to a piece of fruit.
jokes.explanation: An iconic George moment. The explosive 'I'm back, baby! I'm back!' as a declaration of restored virility — triggered by a mango — is both absurd and perfectly in character. It's a great comedic payoff for George's entire storyline in the episode. Hugely quotable.

jokes.text: 'Well, well. If it isn't the first lady of the American theater.' (Jerry greeting Elaine at the door.)
jokes.setup: Elaine comes to return Jerry's things. Jerry is still processing the faking revelation.
jokes.punchline: 'The first lady of the American theater' — a formal, theatrical honorific applied to Elaine in direct reference to her Meryl Streep-level fake orgasm performance.
jokes.explanation: A supremely crafted callback. The theatrical honorific 'first lady of the American theater' applied to Elaine's fake orgasm performance is original, economical, and brilliantly loaded with callbacks to the Meryl Streep thread. It's elegant, savage, and deeply quotable. Among the episode's best lines.

jokes.text: The elaborate exchange of each other's belongings: fins, poker chips, goggles, cards — all itemized with deadpan precision.
jokes.setup: Jerry and Elaine are supposedly just exchanging items.
jokes.punchline: The tedious formality of the item exchange — fins next to goggles, poker chips next to cards — functions as a cold domesticity that makes the impending sex proposition funnier.
jokes.explanation: The scene functions as a slow-burn comedy of social awkwardness. The ritualistic item exchange after a sexual revelation is inherently comic but doesn't have a strong individual punchline — the humor is structural rather than line-based.

jokes.text: Elaine: 'All right, let's go. I'll give you half an hour.' / 'What?' / 'Come on. Jerry, we have to have sex to save the friendship.'
jokes.setup: They've just exchanged items. Jerry asked for sex; Elaine refused. Now, out of nowhere, she reverses.
jokes.punchline: 'We have to have sex to save the friendship.' — The exact inversion of the normal logic (sex destroys friendships), delivered as urgent necessity.
jokes.explanation: A brilliant reversal and one of the episode's best lines. Elaine has completely flipped the logic — where Jerry argued sex won't ruin the friendship, Elaine now argues sex IS NECESSARY TO SAVE it. The inversion is structurally elegant and 'we have to have sex to save the friendship' is one of the most quotable lines in the episode.

jokes.text: 'Sex to save the friendship.' Jerry echoes the phrase in stunned disbelief/acceptance.
jokes.setup: Elaine has just said 'we have to have sex to save the friendship.'
jokes.punchline: Jerry's dazed echo — 'Sex to save the friendship' — the comedic pause before he processes and accepts.
jokes.explanation: The echo works as a reaction beat and adds comedic punctuation to Elaine's line. Like 'Tub is love,' the repetition of the phrase is its own joke. Good but secondary to the line it reflects.

jokes.text: George: 'Very good with the moanings and the gyrations. You really had me going there for a minute.' / Karen: 'You think I was faking?'
jokes.setup: George refuses to believe Karen's genuine response was real.
jokes.punchline: George ironically 'complimenting' Karen on her faking technique — when she wasn't faking — is a perfect reversal of the episode's central premise.
jokes.explanation: A beautiful structural reversal. The episode has been about men being deceived by fake performances — now George, primed by Elaine's confession, disbelieves a REAL performance and condescends about Karen's 'technique.' He's accidentally recreating Elaine's response to Jerry ('Not bad, huh?') from the other direction. Excellent irony.

jokes.text: George: 'Not that I don't appreciate the effort that was put into the performance.' Karen: 'I'd like you to leave.'
jokes.setup: George has just accused Karen of faking and performed an impression of her moans.
jokes.punchline: Karen's cold, formal 'I would like you to leave' — the maximum dignity response to the maximum indignity situation.
jokes.explanation: Karen's formal phrasing ('I would like you to leave') as a response to being accused of faking a genuine orgasm is a great deadpan moment. The formality in context makes it devastating.

jokes.text: 'I can't find my glasses.' / 'Well, hurry up.' / 'Let me look for my glasses.' / 'Get out.' 'Get out.'
jokes.setup: Karen is throwing George out.
jokes.punchline: George's practical glasses-hunting problem contrasting with Karen's increasing fury — the mundane vs. the emotional.
jokes.explanation: A functional cringe escalation. George's inability to find his glasses while being furiously ejected is good physical comedy, though the repeated 'Get out' becomes slightly more funny through sheer insistence.

jokes.text: Jerry tells Elaine the sex failed: 'It's all George's fault. All that talk about impotence...it's a lot of pressure.' Then: 'I'm a little hungry. You wouldn't happen to have any of that mango left?'
jokes.setup: Jerry has also failed sexually with Elaine — the pressure got to him too.
jokes.punchline: Jerry blaming George (mirroring George's 'it's Jerry's fault'), then immediately pivoting to asking for mango — the magical fruit that cured George.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed convergence of two running gags. Jerry mirroring George's blame-deflection ('It's all George's fault' echoing 'It's Jerry's fault') and then immediately seeking the mango cure is elegant multi-thread comedy. The mango request confirms that Jerry takes the aphrodisiac property seriously, which is both funny and character-consistent.

jokes.text: Stand-up: 'The female orgasm...it's kind of like the Batcave. Very few people know where it is. And if you're lucky enough to see it, you probably don't know how you got there...and you can't find your way back after you leave.'
jokes.setup: Jerry performs stand-up on the episode's themes.
jokes.punchline: The Batcave analogy for female orgasm — discovery without navigation, unable to return.
jokes.explanation: A genuinely excellent stand-up bit. The Batcave analogy is original, visually vivid, and perfectly structured. The three-part extension ('you don't know how you got there / can't find your way back') earns each laugh. Among the strongest stand-up material in the episode.

jokes.text: Stand-up: Sex is like a car accident. 'What did you see after the car went out of control?' 'I heard a lot of screeching sounds. I remember I was facing the wrong way at one point. And in the end, my body was thrown clear.'
jokes.setup: Jerry compares male sex awareness to a car accident victim's account.
jokes.punchline: The three-part car accident testimony perfectly maps onto sex chaos: screeching sounds, wrong direction, body thrown clear.
jokes.explanation: The closing stand-up bit is exceptional. The car accident metaphor for male sex experience is original, the three-part structure escalates perfectly, and 'my body was thrown clear' is a magnificent closer. Each element (screeching = sounds, wrong way = positions, thrown clear = post-coital) maps with elegant precision. Strong candidate for best-written joke in the episode even if stand-up is weighted down.

tmdb_overview: Jerry shares George's self-doubt after Elaine admits not all was as it seemed during their romantic relationship.
insight: Season premiere resets with 49 jokes in 22 minutes—Seinfeld's densest opener yet.
